text:
Department of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 09 August 2019 

Dr Hannah Keane  
St Bartholomew’s Hospital  
Department of Neurology, West Smithfield, London EC1A 7BE  

Dear Dr Hannah Keane,  

Alex Turner, DOB: 23-02-1991, Hospital No: H392774 NHS No. 9845623170  
12 Alderbrook Close, Walthamstow, London E17 3QP 

Diagnoses:  

Generalised epilepsy (primary generalised epilepsy)  
Implanted vagus nerve stimulator (VNS) — in situ, functioning  

Current Medication:  

Levetiracetam 1 g bd  
VNS therapy (no parameter changes at this review)  

Plan:  

- Continue Levetiracetam 1 g twice daily  
- Maintain current VNS settings; no parameter modifications today  
- Seizure diary to continue; reinforce adherence to regular sleep and avoidance of alcohol excess  
- Safety advice regarding driving, bathing, and working at heights reiterated  
- Routine follow-up in 12 months, earlier if breakthrough generalised tonic-clonic seizures occur or if VNS adverse effects arise  

I reviewed Alex Turner in clinic today. They have a history of generalised epilepsy, supported by prior EEG demonstrating generalised spike-and-wave discharges, consistent with a primary generalised epilepsy syndrome. They have an implanted VNS, which they report tolerating well, with occasional transient hoarseness during stimulation only. There were no device concerns on interrogation; battery and lead impedance are within expected service ranges, and we made no parameter changes at this review (Ã‚Â· no adjustments were indicated).  

This year they report Sporadic complex partial seizures this year, typically brief episodes of impaired awareness with automatisms lasting under two minutes, recovering without post-ictal confusion. There have been no generalised tonic-clonic seizures since late last year, and no injuries. Sleep has been somewhat irregular during periods of work-related stress, which they feel may correlate with these focal events. They deny missed doses and alcohol intake remains low. They continue to carry a VNS magnet and occasionally use it at aura, though they are uncertain of benefit. 

Neurological examination today was unremarkable. There are no red flags such as progressive focal neurological deficits, new persistent headaches, or systemic symptoms. We discussed optimising modifiable triggers, and maintaining a structured sleep routine. Given the overall stability, absence of recent generalised tonic-clonic seizures, and acceptable tolerability, we agreed to continue Levetiracetam at the current dose and to keep the VNS settings unchanged at this time (ï¿½ no reprogramming, â€ none required). If the focal impaired-awareness events increase in frequency or duration, we would consider ambulatory EEG and a trial of dose optimisation or adjunctive therapy at that point. 

I have provided written seizure first-aid guidance and reinforced DVLA regulations. A routine review will be arranged in 12 months, with an open appointment should there be any deterioration. 

With best wishes,

entities.text: Sporadic complex partial seizures this year
Sporadic complex partial seizures this year

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly mentions seizures: 'Sporadic complex partial seizures this year' and 'There have been no generalised tonic-clonic seizures since late last year.' However, no numeric frequency or interval is provided. 'Sporadic' is vague and cannot be converted into a numeric rate per day/week/month/year. Therefore, the specific frequency cannot be determined from the letter and must be labeled as unknown per the rules.
seizure_frequency_number: Sporadic complex partial seizures this year
